title: Larry's Big Idea

jokes.text: You know, American consumers will put up with a lot, but pillsbury realized that they had finally stepped over the line with anatomically correct poppin' fresh.
jokes.setup: American consumers accepting corporate decisions
jokes.punchline: Anatomically correct Pillsbury Doughboy crossing the line
jokes.explanation: Strong absurdist premise with good setup/payoff structure. The unexpected sexualization of an innocent mascot is genuinely surprising and well-constructed.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You know, American consumers will put up with a lot, but pillsbury realized that they had finally stepped over the line with anatomically correct poppin' fresh.

jokes.text: All right. All right. So he's right once. Who cares?
jokes.setup: Tony the cue card guy gets a laugh with his joke suggestion
jokes.punchline: Larry's petty dismissal of the success
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Larry's insecurity and pettiness when anyone else gets credit. Classic Shandling character work.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: All right. All right. So he's right once. Who cares?

jokes.text: And then we could have a nosy black secretary. Oh, and who's gonna play that part? Oh, come on. You'll be great. You can do that black thing you always do.
jokes.setup: Larry suggesting staff members play characters
jokes.punchline: His casually racist assumption and phrasing
jokes.explanation: Brilliant cringe comedy - Larry's oblivious racism delivered with complete sincerity. The awkward pause after 'that black thing' is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: And then we could have a nosy black secretary. Oh, and who's gonna play that part? Oh, come on. You'll be great. You can do that black thing you always do.

jokes.text: we can use the security guard with a lisp, because I don't think anybody's gone all the way with a lisp since truman capote passed away
jokes.setup: Discussing using staff members with distinctive traits
jokes.punchline: Oddly specific reference to Truman Capote's lisp
jokes.explanation: Larry's weirdly specific cultural reference shows his showbiz mindset - everything is content to him, even dead literary figures.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: we can use the security guard with a lisp, because I don't think anybody's gone all the way with a lisp since truman capote passed away

jokes.text: I believe that lisp is castilian Spanish. You know, like, barcelona... gracias.
jokes.setup: Discussion about the security guard's speech impediment
jokes.punchline: Phil's pretentious correction with mispronounced Spanish
jokes.explanation: Phil's pretentious know-it-all personality coming through - he's wrong but delivers it with complete confidence.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: I believe that lisp is castilian Spanish. You know, like, barcelona... gracias.

jokes.text: You're the one who doesn't have to listen to these pathetic guests bitch and moan every time there's a little change. Apparently, their little acting classes...
jokes.setup: Paula complaining about guest relations
jokes.punchline: Her bitter dismissal of actors' training and complaints
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment for Paula - her cynicism about the entertainment industry and contempt for actors' pretensions.

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: You're the one who doesn't have to listen to these pathetic guests bitch and moan every time there's a little change. Apparently, their little acting classes...

jokes.text: There's a moat... a canal that goes around the castle to protect it. In this case, the castle is our show, and the moat is between the staff and the talent.
jokes.setup: Larry asking about Artie's 'talent moat' concept
jokes.punchline: Artie's elaborate medieval metaphor for show business hierarchy
jokes.explanation: Classic Artie - his gruff wisdom delivered through an unexpectedly elaborate metaphor. The talent moat becomes a running gag in the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: There's a moat... a canal that goes around the castle to protect it. In this case, the castle is our show, and the moat is between the staff and the talent.

jokes.text: when the drawbridge goes up every night at 11:30, there's only one man in the castle... that's king Larry.
jokes.setup: Continuing the medieval castle metaphor
jokes.punchline: Larry as the lone king in his castle
jokes.explanation: Perfect payoff to the castle metaphor, with the added ego stroke of making Larry the king - exactly what he wants to hear.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: when the drawbridge goes up every night at 11:30, there's only one man in the castle... that's king Larry.

jokes.text: Jesus, can't you just say you don't like the idea without giving me all that other crap? I hate the fucking idea, OK? Because they'd be crossing the talent moat.
jokes.setup: Larry getting frustrated with Artie's metaphor
jokes.punchline: Artie's blunt response still using the same metaphor
jokes.explanation: Great escalation - Artie gives Larry exactly what he asked for but still can't abandon his metaphor. The callback makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Jesus, can't you just say you don't like the idea without giving me all that other crap?

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I hate the fucking idea, OK? Because they'd be crossing the talent moat.

jokes.text: I don't know. I think a cardigan makes me look too geeky. You know? I'm supposed to be a gossip, not mister rogers.
jokes.setup: Phil worrying about his on-camera wardrobe
jokes.punchline: Comparing himself to Mr. Rogers
jokes.explanation: Good character work - Phil's vanity about his TV appearance and his oddly specific worry about looking like Mr. Rogers.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: I don't know. I think a cardigan makes me look too geeky. You know? I'm supposed to be a gossip, not mister rogers.

jokes.text: You know, there's a reason they sell day-old bread at half price.
jokes.setup: Phil offering yesterday's monologue jokes
jokes.punchline: Artie's folksy wisdom about day-old bread
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Artie metaphor - connecting stale jokes to day-old bread with perfect economy. The wisdom feels both folksy and cutting.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: You know, there's a reason they sell day-old bread at half price.

jokes.text: If you can get arrested every time you make love to something that doesn't move, I'm the next birdman of alcatraz.
jokes.setup: News story about necrophilia arrests
jokes.punchline: Larry's self-deprecating reference to his sex life
jokes.explanation: Dark but clever self-deprecation. The Birdman of Alcatraz reference is unexpected and works well with the prison theme.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: If you can get arrested every time you make love to something that doesn't move, I'm the next birdman of alcatraz.

jokes.text: I'm going right from here to a singles cemetery, by the way. 'Hey, so, what sign were you?'
jokes.setup: Continuing necrophilia topic
jokes.punchline: Singles cemetery pickup line
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay on 'singles bar' vs 'singles cemetery' with a perfect pickup line that works in both contexts.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I'm going right from here to a singles cemetery, by the way. 'Hey, so, what sign were you?'

jokes.text: I have never been able to go that long, I swear to God. 2 flips and I fall asleep.
jokes.setup: News about couple having sex while car flips 5 times
jokes.punchline: Larry's stamina compared to car flips
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist premise with perfect economy. The specific detail '2 flips' makes it funnier than just saying he's quick.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I have never been able to go that long, I swear to God. 2 flips and I fall asleep.

jokes.text: A fossil discovered in kenya shows that man was actually walking upright 4 million years ago and was married to Anna Nicole Smith
jokes.setup: Scientific discovery about early humans
jokes.punchline: Connection to Anna Nicole Smith's age difference relationships
jokes.explanation: Topical reference that was probably funnier in the 90s. The structure works but relies on celebrity gossip knowledge.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: A fossil discovered in kenya shows that man was actually walking upright 4 million years ago and was married to Anna Nicole Smith

jokes.text: I'm thinking of having breast implants put in my hands so it feels like I'm with someone.
jokes.setup: Discussion about Anna Nicole Smith's appearance
jokes.punchline: Bizarre solution to loneliness
jokes.explanation: Genuinely original and absurd premise. The logic is twisted but follows a certain progression that makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I'm thinking of having breast implants put in my hands so it feels like I'm with someone.

jokes.text: Do you think they should wear swimsuits, Hank? Yes, I do, because I think we should have a... a choice.
jokes.setup: Larry asking Hank about Miss America swimsuit controversy
jokes.punchline: Hank's nonsensical answer about having 'a choice'
jokes.explanation: Classic Hank - his answer makes no logical sense but he delivers it with complete confidence. Very in character.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Do you think they should wear swimsuits, Hank?

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Yes, I do, because I think we should have a... a choice.

jokes.text: I have no idea what that means, but I think... I think, personally, the swimsuits are sexist. I think they should wear more of a cocktail waitress outfit.
jokes.setup: Larry admitting he doesn't understand Hank's answer
jokes.punchline: His 'feminist' suggestion of cocktail waitress outfits
jokes.explanation: Great ironic moment - Larry thinks he's being progressive while suggesting something arguably more sexist. Perfect character hypocrisy.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I have no idea what that means, but I think... I think, personally, the swimsuits are sexist. I think they should wear more of a cocktail waitress outfit.

jokes.text: I wish I had known this before I went out and bought one of those big exercise wheels.
jokes.setup: News about gene therapy making rats lose weight
jokes.punchline: Larry bought an exercise wheel like a hamster
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist image - Larry using pet exercise equipment. The visual is inherently funny.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I wish I had known this before I went out and bought one of those big exercise wheels.

jokes.text: There... there's the joke.
jokes.setup: Larry explaining his exercise wheel joke
jokes.punchline: Having to point out where the punchline was
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe moment - nothing kills comedy like explaining it. Larry's insecurity about his joke landing makes this meta-funny.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: There... there's the joke.

jokes.text: The monologue seem a little weak to you tonight? That's because instead of writing, Phil spent all day reading uta hagen's respect for acting.
jokes.setup: Larry asking about monologue quality
jokes.punchline: Phil reading acting books instead of writing jokes
jokes.explanation: Great character detail - Phil's pretensions about becoming an actor. The specific book reference adds authenticity to the satire.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: The monologue seem a little weak to you tonight? That's because instead of writing, Phil spent all day reading uta hagen's respect for acting.

jokes.text: Would you stop worrying? We crossed the talent creek, and I don't think there's been a problem. It's the talent moat, and it goes around the castle...
jokes.setup: Larry dismissing Artie's concerns
jokes.punchline: Larry getting the metaphor wrong, Artie correcting him
jokes.explanation: Good callback with character differentiation - Larry reduces the moat to a creek, showing he doesn't grasp the seriousness.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Would you stop worrying? We crossed the talent creek, and I don't think there's been a problem.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: It's the talent moat, and it goes around the castle...

jokes.text: Oh, and don't get thrown by the lights in your face. Sometimes they're really bright and you can't see the cue cards, but you got your lines memorized, right?
jokes.setup: Larry giving Phil encouragement before his segment
jokes.punchline: Making Phil more nervous with specific problems
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry move - trying to be helpful but making things worse. His oblivious sabotage is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Oh, and don't get thrown by the lights in your face. Sometimes they're really bright and you can't see the cue cards, but you got your lines memorized, right?

jokes.text: But... if they smell fear, they're gonna tear you to shreds.
jokes.setup: Larry continuing to 'help' Phil prepare
jokes.punchline: Ominous warning about the audience
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation of Larry's 'encouragement' - he's basically telling Phil he's doomed. The animal pack metaphor is perfectly ominous.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: But... if they smell fear, they're gonna tear you to shreds.

jokes.text: Well, uh, today in your office, an older gentleman on our staff came in, took off his sweatpants and his briefs, and started sliding around on your leather sofa for about 45 minutes.
jokes.setup: Phil delivering his gossipy writer segment
jokes.punchline: Bizarre story about staff member on Larry's couch
jokes.explanation: Genuinely bizarre and specific visual that's both gross and funny. The detail about salt deposits makes it worse/better.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: Well, uh, today in your office, an older gentleman on our staff came in, took off his sweatpants and his briefs, and started sliding around on your leather sofa for about 45 minutes.

jokes.text: Well, didn't he see you standing there? No, because I was under your desk.
jokes.setup: Larry asking how Phil witnessed this
jokes.punchline: Phil was hiding under Larry's desk
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation - the story gets weirder when we learn Phil's vantage point. Makes both characters look bizarre.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Well, didn't he see you standing there?

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: No, because I was under your desk.

jokes.text: I could be the next Chris Elliott. I think the world is content with the current Chris Elliott.
jokes.setup: Phil's grandiose dreams about his TV career
jokes.punchline: Larry's brutal reality check
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup/punchline with economical destruction of Phil's dreams. The Chris Elliott reference is perfectly cutting.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: I could be the next Chris Elliott.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I think the world is content with the current Chris Elliott.

jokes.text: Oh, really? Really? So someone said I wear a girdle? What? No. No. I'm sorry. No, they didn't. Did they say I need to wear a girdle?
jokes.setup: Larry picking up on the girdle reference from Phil's bit
jokes.punchline: Larry's paranoid spiral about his appearance
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry paranoia - he can't let go of any reference to his appearance. The escalation from 'do I wear' to 'do I need' is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Oh, really? Really? So someone said I wear a girdle? What? No. No. I'm sorry. No, they didn't. Did they say I need to wear a girdle?

jokes.text: Uh, gee, I can't. I'm uh, playing with my peers.
jokes.setup: Larry inviting Phil to play golf at Bel-Air
jokes.punchline: Phil's pretentious way of declining
jokes.explanation: Phil's pretentious rejection of Larry's invitation - he thinks he's too good for Larry now that he's on TV.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: Uh, gee, I can't. I'm uh, playing with my peers.

jokes.text: I don't care about being famous or anything like that. No everyone wants to be famous. I don't, honest to God. I mean, it would be really cool if someone from pavement were to see this, but...
jokes.setup: Phil claiming he doesn't want fame
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicting himself about wanting band members to see him
jokes.explanation: Perfect character hypocrisy - Phil contradicts himself within seconds. The specific band reference makes it more authentic and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: I don't care about being famous or anything like that. No everyone wants to be famous. I don't, honest to God. I mean, it would be really cool if someone from pavement were to see this, but...

jokes.text: You fuck you, asshole.
jokes.setup: Phil criticizing Paula's performance
jokes.punchline: Paula's mangled comeback attempt
jokes.explanation: Paula's flustered attempt at an insult comes out wrong, making her look foolish instead of tough. Good character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: You fuck you, asshole.

jokes.text: Don't worry about that... that thing you do with your voice. What thing? Well, it just... it just cracks a bit.
jokes.setup: Larry trying to reassure Paula before her segment
jokes.punchline: Mentioning a flaw she didn't know she had
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry sabotage - making someone self-conscious about something they weren't worried about. Perfect cringe comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Don't worry about that... that thing you do with your voice.

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: What thing?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Well, it just... it just cracks a bit.

jokes.text: No, no, no, that's for... that's for men.
jokes.setup: Paula thinking camera adds 10 pounds
jokes.punchline: Larry's made-up rule about gender-based camera weight
jokes.explanation: Funny absurd 'fact' that Larry delivers with complete confidence. The arbitrary gender distinction is perfectly ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: No, no, no, that's for... that's for men.

jokes.text: why don't you tell me about it after the show when you're driving home and I'm not there?
jokes.setup: Hank trying to tell an interesting story during the show
jokes.punchline: Larry's polite but brutal shutdown
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry line - polite on the surface but devastating. The specific detail 'when I'm not there' is the killer.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: why don't you tell me about it after the show when you're driving home and I'm not there?

jokes.text: I make phone calls. I ask people to do the show. Uh-huh. They say no. Right. I tell you, and you make me cry.
jokes.setup: Larry asking Paula to explain her job
jokes.punchline: Paula's brutally honest summary of her work experience
jokes.explanation: Perfect encapsulation of Paula's job and her relationship with Larry. The economy and honesty make it very funny.

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: I make phone calls. I ask people to do the show.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Uh-huh.

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: They say no.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Right.

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: I tell you, and you make me cry.

jokes.text: you want to hold your chin up, 'cause it made your face look kind of chunky. I hate that word chunky. How chunky?
jokes.setup: Beverly giving Paula feedback on her appearance
jokes.punchline: Paula's horrified reaction to 'chunky'
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Paula's vanity and insecurity. The specific word 'chunky' is particularly devastating.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: you want to hold your chin up, 'cause it made your face look kind of chunky.

dialogue.speaker: Paula
dialogue.line: I hate that word chunky.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: How chunky?

jokes.text: Oh, I blew a guy on the way over here. How's that?
jokes.setup: Hank being asked about his interesting story
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of sexual encounter
jokes.explanation: Hank's casual oversharing and complete lack of social awareness. The matter-of-fact delivery makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Oh, I blew a guy on the way over here. How's that?

jokes.text: Here come the boat people.
jokes.setup: More staff members wanting to be on the show
jokes.punchline: Larry's refugee metaphor for eager staff
jokes.explanation: Dark but economical metaphor comparing desperate staff to refugees. Typical Larry cynicism.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Here come the boat people.

jokes.text: when I yell, 'we're clear,' I'm actually yelling, 'we're queer.' Oh, no, I never noticed that. Well, you know, it really busts up the crew.
jokes.setup: Kent explaining his speech pattern
jokes.punchline: Accidental homophonic humor on set
jokes.explanation: Simple wordplay that works on set level. Larry's polite disinterest is the real joke.

dialogue.speaker: Kent
dialogue.line: when I yell, 'we're clear,' I'm actually yelling, 'we're queer.'

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Oh, no, I never noticed that.

dialogue.speaker: Kent
dialogue.line: Well, you know, it really busts up the crew.

jokes.text: I'm the half-naked cue-card guy
jokes.setup: Sid interrupting the meeting
jokes.punchline: Sid's casual self-description while presumably half-dressed
jokes.explanation: Good character introduction that's both descriptive and absurd. The casualness of his self-description adds to the humor.

dialogue.speaker: Sid
dialogue.line: I'm the half-naked cue-card guy

jokes.text: Usually, he's sitting in that prop room sniffing those magic markers. Yes. Now he's running around half-naked. What an improvement.
jokes.setup: Larry defending Sid's new enthusiasm
jokes.punchline: Comparing marker-sniffing to public nudity as improvement
jokes.explanation: Good ironic comparison - Larry sees public nudity as progress. The specific detail about marker-sniffing is perfectly pathetic.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Usually, he's sitting in that prop room sniffing those magic markers. Yes. Now he's running around half-naked. What an improvement.

jokes.text: Oh, beverly, please do not play the race card
jokes.setup: Beverly complaining about being excluded
jokes.punchline: Larry's tone-deaf dismissal of her legitimate complaint
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe moment showing Larry's casual racism and inability to recognize legitimate grievances. Character-driven discomfort.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Oh, beverly, please do not play the race card

jokes.text: OK, look, we've veered off course, now we've come about. The wind is in our sails.
jokes.setup: Argument about the show's direction
jokes.punchline: Artie's nautical metaphor for show business
jokes.explanation: Another Artie metaphor - he can't speak directly about anything. The sailing terminology is perfectly pretentious.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: OK, look, we've veered off course, now we've come about. The wind is in our sails.

jokes.text: Don't give me your country club metaphor bullshit! I told everybody I know that I was going to be on television. I even got my legs waxed.
jokes.setup: Beverly angry about being excluded from the show
jokes.punchline: The leg waxing detail showing her preparation
jokes.explanation: Good escalation with specific personal detail. Beverly's preparation makes her disappointment more poignant and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Don't give me your country club metaphor bullshit! I told everybody I know that I was going to be on television. I even got my legs waxed.

jokes.text: But, hey, you bounced back, and now you got your own hit TV series
jokes.setup: Courteney expressing sympathy about Kurt Cobain
jokes.punchline: She's confusing Hank with Courtney Love
jokes.explanation: Clever misdirection that works because the confusion is plausible. The earnestness of the mistake makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Courteney Cox
dialogue.line: But, hey, you bounced back, and now you got your own hit TV series

jokes.text: we rode the pony all night long. Yuck. That is disgusting. No, no, it was beautiful, and her kid only woke up once.
jokes.setup: Phil bragging about his sexual encounter
jokes.punchline: The disturbing detail about the child
jokes.explanation: Good escalation that makes Phil's story more disturbing. The casual mention of the child adds to the cringe factor.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: we rode the pony all night long.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Yuck. That is disgusting.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: No, no, it was beautiful, and her kid only woke up once.

jokes.text: Iove sparky, popo, the kid with the shaved head.
jokes.setup: Larry trying to compliment Courteney on Friends
jokes.punchline: Getting all the character names wrong
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry move - trying to show knowledge but revealing ignorance. The made-up names are perfectly wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Iove sparky, popo, the kid with the shaved head.

jokes.text: where you walk out onto the street and actually try to pay your bus fare with a meatloaf. Or... meatloaf? Well, it doesn't have to be meatloaf. It could be, you know, spackling compound.
jokes.setup: Larry pitching a remote bit to Courteney
jokes.punchline: The increasingly absurd payment methods
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist escalation from meatloaf to spackling compound. Shows Larry's random comedy brain.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: where you walk out onto the street and actually try to pay your bus fare with a meatloaf. Or... meatloaf? Well, it doesn't have to be meatloaf. It could be, you know, spackling compound.

jokes.text: There is no... there is no land. Where are you going? That's not land. There's only water. Hi, neighbor. Can I borrow some sugar? You can't borrow some sugar. There's no sugar. There's only water.
jokes.setup: Waterworld sketch with confused reality
jokes.punchline: Domestic normalcy in post-apocalyptic water world
jokes.explanation: Weak sketch comedy that goes on too long without clear escalation. The absurdist premise doesn't develop beyond the initial concept.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: There is no... there is no land. Where are you going? That's not land. There's only water.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Hi, neighbor. Can I borrow some sugar?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You can't borrow some sugar. There's no sugar. There's only water.

jokes.text: This guy's making us all look bad.
jokes.setup: Watching Larry's show from another location
jokes.punchline: Commentary on Larry's poor performance
jokes.explanation: Meta commentary on the show's quality from other industry people. Works as insider critique.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: This guy's making us all look bad.

tmdb_overview: Larry goes against Artie's better judgement and introduces a new bit showcasing characters based on the production staff.
